title: First vowels: a, i, u
pedagogy: Vowels (1/4): the three core short/long pairs a (◌ะ/◌า), i (◌ิ/◌ี), u (◌ุ/◌ู). Concepts: vowels attach around the consonant (after/above/below); short vs long length, which later decides live/dead.

exercises.title: Vowels wrap around consonants
exercises.thaiExample: กา กิ กุ
body: Thai vowels never stand alone — each one attaches to a consonant: after it (กา), above it (กิ), below it (กุ), or even before it.
In this course the dotted circle ◌ shows where the consonant sits: ◌า means "the vowel า, written after".
Always read the consonant first, then the vowel — wherever the vowel is written.

exercises.title: Short and long
body: Almost every Thai vowel comes as a short/long pair: กะ (ka, clipped) versus กา (kaa, held).
Length changes meaning — and soon it will help decide a syllable's tone: short-vowel syllables are "dead", long open ones are "live".
Listen for duration, not a different vowel sound.
